title: Optimal income taxation when asset taxation is limited
summary_en: A classic question in public finance: when government can't tax accumulated wealth directly, how should it tax income? This paper works out the answer in a general dynamic setting where the household cares about consumption smoothing but the government cannot observe or tax assets. The resulting optimal income-tax schedule differs from the textbook benchmarks — the inability to tax assets forces the income tax to do more work. The paper provides a tractable characterisation and relates the optimal design to observable features of household preferences and asset-return distributions. A foundational result for optimal-taxation theory in practically-constrained settings.
data_used: Theoretical paper; no empirical data (some illustrative calibrations).
policy_relevance: Relevant to Hungary's tax-policy design, especially where asset taxation is politically or administratively limited. The paper provides a rigorous foundation for how to set the income-tax schedule when wealth-tax alternatives are limited — a frequent constraint in real-world reform design. Relevant to Pénzügyminisztérium tax-design debates. Caveat: the framework is general but requires empirical parameterisation before generating specific Hungarian policy numbers.
